question_text: Total Revenue (TR) is calculated as:
explanation: Total Revenue is the total amount of money a firm receives from selling its output. TR = Price × Quantity sold (TR = P × Q). For example, if a firm sells 100 units at ₹50 each, TR = 50 × 100 = ₹5,000. TR depends on both the price per unit and the number of units sold.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: TC + Profit = TR is an accounting identity, but the DEFINITION of TR is Price × Quantity. The formula P × Q directly calculates revenue from sales data.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing an accounting identity with the direct definition.

elimination_hints.hint: AR × MR has no standard economic meaning. TR = P × Q, or equivalently TR = AR × Q (since AR = P). Don't multiply AR by MR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating an incorrect formula by multiplying two derived revenue concepts.

elimination_hints.hint: P ÷ Q would give price per unit of quantity, which is meaningless. TR = P × Q (multiplication, not division).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing multiplication with division in the TR formula.

question_text: Average Revenue (AR) is:
explanation: Average Revenue is the revenue received per unit of output sold. AR = TR/Q = (P × Q)/Q = P. Therefore, Average Revenue equals the PRICE of the product. The AR curve is the same as the demand curve faced by the firm. Under perfect competition, AR is constant (horizontal demand curve). Under imperfect competition, AR falls as more is sold (downward-sloping demand curve).
subject: economics

options.text: Revenue per unit of output, which equals the price (TR/Q = P)
Change in TR when one more unit is sold

elimination_hints.hint: TR × Q is meaningless. AR = TR/Q (division, not multiplication). AR = TR/Q = (P×Q)/Q = P.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing multiplication with division in the AR formula.

elimination_hints.hint: Change in TR from one more unit sold is Marginal Revenue (MR = ΔTR/ΔQ), not Average Revenue. AR = TR/Q = Price.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Average Revenue with Marginal Revenue.

elimination_hints.hint: TR − TC is Profit, not Average Revenue. AR is revenue per unit: AR = TR/Q = Price.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing revenue per unit with profit.

question_text: Marginal Revenue (MR) is defined as:
explanation: Marginal Revenue is the additional revenue earned by selling one more unit of output. MR = ΔTR/ΔQ = change in total revenue divided by change in quantity. For example, if TR from selling 5 units is ₹500 and TR from selling 6 units is ₹580, MR of the 6th unit = 580 − 500 = ₹80. Under perfect competition, MR = AR = Price (constant). Under imperfect competition, MR < AR and MR falls faster than AR.
subject: economics

options.text: Additional revenue from selling one more unit (ΔTR/ΔQ)
Revenue earned from the first unit sold

elimination_hints.hint: TR/Q is Average Revenue (AR), not Marginal Revenue. MR is the CHANGE in TR from selling one additional unit: MR = ΔTR/ΔQ.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing AR formula with MR formula.

elimination_hints.hint: TR × P is meaningless. MR = ΔTR/ΔQ, which measures the additional revenue from one more unit of sales.
elimination_hints.misconception: Creating an incorrect formula.

elimination_hints.hint: Revenue from the first unit is the price of the first unit, which may equal MR of the first unit. But MR is defined for ANY additional unit, not just the first. MR = ΔTR/ΔQ for each successive unit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Restricting MR to only the first unit instead of understanding it as marginal (additional) for any unit.

question_text: Under perfect competition, the relationship between AR and MR is:
explanation: Under perfect competition, a firm is a price-taker — it can sell any quantity at the prevailing market price. Since price is constant, every additional unit adds the same amount (price) to total revenue. Therefore, MR = AR = Price. Both the AR and MR curves are the same horizontal straight line at the market price level. TR increases at a constant rate (TR is a straight line from the origin with slope = price).
subject: economics

options.text: AR = MR = Price, and both are constant (horizontal line)
MR is always less than AR
MR is always greater than AR

elimination_hints.hint: MR < AR is true under IMPERFECT competition (monopoly, monopolistic competition) where the firm must lower price to sell more. Under perfect competition, the firm sells at a constant price, so MR = AR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the imperfect competition AR-MR relationship to perfect competition.

elimination_hints.hint: MR can never be greater than AR under any market structure. At best, MR = AR (perfect competition). Under imperfect competition, MR < AR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the AR-MR inequality.

elimination_hints.hint: Under perfect competition, neither AR nor MR changes — both are constant at the market price. The firm is a price-taker and cannot influence the price.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying a dynamic relationship where none exists in perfect competition.

question_text: Under monopoly or imperfect competition, the MR curve:
explanation: Under monopoly/imperfect competition, the firm faces a downward-sloping demand curve (AR curve). To sell more, it must LOWER the price — not just on the additional unit, but on ALL units sold. So MR < AR (price) for every unit after the first. The MR curve lies below the AR curve and falls twice as fast (for a linear demand curve, MR bisects the distance between the AR curve and the Y-axis). MR can even become negative when TR starts declining.
subject: economics

options.text: Lies below the AR curve and falls faster than AR

elimination_hints.hint: MR = AR only under perfect competition. Under monopoly, the firm must lower price to sell more, causing MR to fall below AR. They coincide only at the first unit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the perfect competition result to monopoly.

elimination_hints.hint: MR can NEVER be above AR. Since lowering price for one more unit also reduces revenue on all previous units, the marginal gain (MR) is always less than the price (AR).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding why MR must be below AR under imperfect competition.

elimination_hints.hint: A horizontal MR line occurs only under perfect competition (price-taker). Under monopoly, the firm faces a downward-sloping demand curve, so MR also slopes downward.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying perfect competition characteristics to monopoly.

question_text: When MR = 0, Total Revenue (TR) is:
explanation: When MR = 0, selling one more unit adds NOTHING to total revenue. This means TR has reached its maximum point. Before this point, MR > 0, so TR was still rising. After this point, MR becomes negative, so TR starts declining. The MR = 0 point marks the peak of the TR curve. This corresponds to the mid-point of the linear demand (AR) curve, where price elasticity of demand = 1 (unit elastic).
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: TR is NOT zero when MR = 0. TR is the cumulative sum of all marginal revenues. MR = 0 just means the LAST unit added nothing; previous units contributed positively. TR is at its peak.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing MR = 0 (no additional revenue) with TR = 0 (no revenue at all).

elimination_hints.hint: TR is at its MAXIMUM, not minimum, when MR = 0. MR = 0 is the turning point where TR stops rising and is about to fall.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing maximum with minimum.

elimination_hints.hint: TR is rising when MR > 0. When MR = 0, TR has stopped rising and reached its peak. TR neither rises nor falls at this exact point.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that MR = 0 means TR is at a turning point, not still rising.

question_text: A firm sells 10 units at ₹20 each. To sell 11 units, it must reduce the price to ₹19. What is the MR of the 11th unit?
explanation: TR at 10 units = 10 × 20 = ₹200. TR at 11 units = 11 × 19 = ₹209. MR of 11th unit = ΔTR = 209 − 200 = ₹9. Note that MR (₹9) is LESS than the new price (₹19). This is because lowering the price from ₹20 to ₹19 loses ₹1 on each of the original 10 units (loss of ₹10), but gains ₹19 from the 11th unit. Net gain = 19 − 10 = ₹9.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: ₹19 is the new price (AR), not MR. MR < AR under imperfect competition. MR = TR₁₁ − TR₁₀ = (11×19) − (10×20) = 209 − 200 = ₹9.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing MR with the price (AR) of the additional unit.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹20 was the old price before the reduction. MR is calculated from the change in TR: 209 − 200 = ₹9.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the old price instead of calculating the actual change in TR.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹209 is the new Total Revenue, not MR. MR is the CHANGE: 209 − 200 = ₹9.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reporting TR instead of MR.

question_text: The AR curve of a firm is also its:
explanation: The AR curve IS the demand curve faced by the firm. Since AR = TR/Q = (P×Q)/Q = P, the AR curve shows the price at which each quantity can be sold. This is exactly what a demand curve does — it shows the relationship between price and quantity demanded. Under perfect competition, the AR (demand) curve is horizontal. Under monopoly, it slopes downward.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: The supply curve is related to MC (above AVC), not AR. The AR curve shows the price-quantity relationship from the buyer's side — which is the demand curve.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing AR (demand) with supply.

elimination_hints.hint: MC is a cost concept showing the cost of producing one more unit. AR shows the revenue (price) per unit — a completely different concept.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing revenue curves with cost curves.

elimination_hints.hint: AC shows the cost per unit of output, while AR shows the revenue (price) per unit. They are compared to determine profit/loss, but they are not the same curve.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing average cost with average revenue.

question_text: For a linear demand curve (AR), the MR curve:
explanation: For a linear (straight-line) demand curve, the MR curve is also a straight line that starts from the same point on the Y-axis as the AR curve but has TWICE the slope. Geometrically, the MR curve bisects the horizontal distance between the Y-axis and the demand (AR) curve. If the demand curve equation is P = a − bQ, then MR = a − 2bQ. Both have the same Y-intercept (a) but MR has double the slope (2b vs b).
subject: economics

options.text: Has the same Y-intercept as AR but twice the slope, bisecting the horizontal distance
Is parallel to the AR curve at a fixed distance below
Is always perpendicular to the AR curve

elimination_hints.hint: MR is NOT parallel to AR. Parallel lines have the same slope. MR has twice the slope of AR (steeper), so they diverge — the gap between them increases as Q increases.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking MR is a fixed distance below AR rather than having double the slope.

elimination_hints.hint: Perpendicular would mean the MR curve is at a right angle to the demand curve, which is geometrically incorrect. MR has the same intercept and double the slope.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly describing the geometric relationship between AR and MR.

elimination_hints.hint: MR starts from the same Y-intercept as AR (not the origin). When Q = 0, MR = AR = the price intercept (a). Only TR starts from the origin.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the starting point of MR with the starting point of TR.

question_text: A firm in perfect competition earns normal profit when:
explanation: Normal profit is the minimum profit needed to keep a firm in the industry — it is included in the cost (as the opportunity cost of entrepreneurship). Normal profit occurs when TR = TC, or equivalently AR = AC. At this point, the firm earns zero supernormal profit but still earns enough to cover all costs including the entrepreneur's opportunity cost. If AR > AC, the firm earns supernormal profit. If AR < AC, it incurs losses.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: When AR > AC, the firm earns SUPERNORMAL (abnormal/economic) profit — revenue per unit exceeds cost per unit. Normal profit requires AR = AC (break-even).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing supernormal profit with normal profit.

elimination_hints.hint: When AR < AC, the firm incurs LOSSES — cost per unit exceeds revenue per unit. The firm is below break-even, not at normal profit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing losses with normal profit.

elimination_hints.hint: MR > MC indicates the firm should INCREASE output (each additional unit adds more to revenue than to cost). It doesn't indicate normal profit — that requires AR = AC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the output-adjustment condition with the profit condition.

question_text: If a firm sells 8 units at ₹25 each, what is the Total Revenue?
explanation: TR = Price × Quantity = 25 × 8 = ₹200. Each unit is sold at ₹25, and 8 units are sold, so total money received = ₹200. AR = TR/Q = 200/8 = ₹25 (which equals the price, as expected).
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: ₹25 is the price per unit (AR), not Total Revenue. TR = P × Q = 25 × 8 = ₹200.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reporting price instead of total revenue.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹33 (≈ 25 + 8) is the sum of price and quantity, which is meaningless. TR = P × Q (multiply, don't add).
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding price and quantity instead of multiplying.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹3.125 (= 25/8) divides price by quantity, which is meaningless. TR = P × Q = 25 × 8 = ₹200.
elimination_hints.misconception: Dividing instead of multiplying.

question_text: Under perfect competition, the TR curve is:
explanation: Under perfect competition, price is constant (the firm is a price-taker). Since TR = P × Q and P is constant, TR increases proportionally with Q. This makes the TR curve a straight line starting from the origin, with slope equal to the constant market price. Each additional unit adds the same amount (P) to TR, so the rate of increase is constant.
subject: economics

options.text: A straight line through the origin with a constant positive slope

elimination_hints.hint: An inverted U-shaped TR curve occurs under IMPERFECT competition (monopoly), where price must be reduced to sell more. Under perfect competition, price is constant, so TR rises linearly.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying monopoly TR behaviour to perfect competition.

elimination_hints.hint: A horizontal line would mean TR is constant regardless of output. This describes the AR (price) line under perfect competition, not the TR curve. TR = P × Q rises with Q.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the AR curve (horizontal under PC) with the TR curve.

elimination_hints.hint: TR cannot slope downward under perfect competition. Since price is positive and constant, selling more units always increases total revenue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking TR can decline when price is constant and positive.

question_text: Under monopoly, the TR curve is:
explanation: Under monopoly, the firm faces a downward-sloping demand curve — it must lower price to sell more. Initially, the gain from selling more units outweighs the loss from the price reduction, so TR rises. But eventually, the price reductions become too large, and TR starts falling. This creates an inverted U-shape (hill shape). TR is maximum when MR = 0, and declines when MR becomes negative.
subject: economics

options.text: A straight line from the origin
An inverted U-shape — first rises, reaches a maximum, then falls

elimination_hints.hint: A straight-line TR from the origin occurs under PERFECT competition where price is constant. Under monopoly, price changes as quantity changes, making TR non-linear.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying perfect competition TR behaviour to monopoly.

elimination_hints.hint: Horizontal TR would mean revenue doesn't change with output, which contradicts the monopolist's ability to earn more by selling more (at least initially).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing horizontal AR with horizontal TR.

elimination_hints.hint: TR does not decline from the start. Initially, even a monopolist gains more from selling additional units (MR > 0). TR declines only after MR becomes negative.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the initial phase where selling more increases TR.

question_text: Under perfect competition, AR = MR = Price.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Under perfect competition, the firm is a price-taker — it can sell any quantity at the prevailing market price without affecting it. Since price is constant: AR = TR/Q = P×Q/Q = P, and MR = ΔTR/ΔQ = P (since each additional unit sold at the same price adds P to revenue). Therefore, AR = MR = Price, and all three are represented by the same horizontal line.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is true. Under perfect competition, the firm sells every unit at the same market price, so revenue per unit (AR) = additional revenue per unit (MR) = Price. This is a defining feature of perfect competition.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the imperfect competition result (MR < AR) to perfect competition.

question_text: A monopolist's demand schedule shows: at P = ₹50, Q = 10; at P = ₹45, Q = 11. The MR of the 11th unit is:
explanation: TR at Q=10: 50 × 10 = ₹500. TR at Q=11: 45 × 11 = ₹495. MR = ΔTR = 495 − 500 = −₹5. The MR is NEGATIVE because the revenue loss from reducing price on all 10 existing units (₹5 × 10 = ₹50 loss) exceeds the revenue gained from the 11th unit (₹45 gain). Net change = 45 − 50 = −₹5. This shows that the monopolist has passed the point of maximum TR.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: ₹45 is the new price (AR), not MR. Under monopoly, MR < AR. MR = TR₁₁ − TR₁₀ = 495 − 500 = −₹5.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing price with marginal revenue.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50 was the original price. MR = change in TR = (45×11) − (50×10) = 495 − 500 = −₹5.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the old price as MR.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5 is the price reduction (50 − 45), not the MR. MR accounts for the impact of this price cut on ALL units: MR = 495 − 500 = −₹5.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the price change with MR, ignoring the revenue loss on existing units.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Under monopoly, MR is always less than AR (except for the first unit).
Reason (R): To sell an additional unit, a monopolist must lower the price not only for the extra unit but for all previous units as well.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. Under monopoly, the firm faces a downward-sloping demand curve. To sell one more unit, it must reduce the price for ALL units (assuming no price discrimination). So while it gains revenue from the extra unit at the new (lower) price, it loses revenue on all previous units due to the price cut. This net effect makes MR < AR for every unit after the first. R correctly identifies this mechanism.
subject: economics

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R IS the correct explanation. The reason MR < AR is precisely because the price cut affects all units, not just the marginal one. This revenue loss on previous units makes MR fall below the new price (AR).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising the direct causal link between uniform price reduction and MR < AR.

elimination_hints.hint: R is true. Under monopoly (without price discrimination), the firm charges a single price for all units. To sell more, it must lower this uniform price for ALL units.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the uniform pricing constraint under monopoly.

elimination_hints.hint: A is true. MR < AR under monopoly is a mathematical certainty (for all units after the first) given the downward-sloping demand curve and uniform pricing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the well-established MR < AR result under monopoly.

question_text: A profit-maximising firm produces at the output level where:
explanation: A profit-maximising firm produces at the output where MC = MR. This is because: if MR > MC, producing one more unit adds more to revenue than to cost → profit increases → produce more. If MR < MC, the last unit costs more than it earns → profit decreases → produce less. Profit is maximised where MR = MC (and MC is rising). This rule applies to ALL market structures — perfect competition, monopoly, monopolistic competition, and oligopoly.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: Maximum TR (where MR = 0) does not maximise profit. At that point, MC is positive, so the firm is producing beyond the profit-maximising level. Profit max requires MR = MC, not MR = 0.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing revenue maximisation with profit maximisation.

elimination_hints.hint: AR (price) doesn't have a 'maximum' under perfect competition (it's constant) and maximising price isn't the goal — maximising profit is. The firm should produce where MR = MC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing price maximisation with profit maximisation.

elimination_hints.hint: Minimum AC gives the most efficient output level but not necessarily the profit-maximising one. Profit max occurs where MR = MC, which may be at a higher output than minimum AC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cost efficiency (min AC) with profit maximisation (MR = MC).

question_text: If TR at 7 units is ₹350 and TR at 8 units is ₹376, then MR of the 8th unit is:
explanation: MR = ΔTR/ΔQ = (TR₈ − TR₇)/(8 − 7) = (376 − 350)/1 = ₹26. The 8th unit adds ₹26 to total revenue. Note: AR of 8 units = 376/8 = ₹47, while MR = ₹26. Since MR < AR, this firm is operating under imperfect competition (price must be reduced to sell more).
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: ₹376 is the TR at 8 units, not MR. MR = TR₈ − TR₇ = 376 − 350 = ₹26.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reporting TR instead of MR.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹47 is the AR at 8 units (376/8 = 47). MR is the change in TR: 376 − 350 = ₹26.
elimination_hints.misconception: Calculating AR instead of MR.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹350 is the TR at 7 units. MR of the 8th unit = TR₈ − TR₇ = 376 − 350 = ₹26.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reporting the previous TR instead of calculating the change.

question_text: When MR is negative, it means:
explanation: Negative MR means that selling one more unit actually REDUCES total revenue. This happens under imperfect competition when the price reduction needed to sell the extra unit causes such a large revenue loss on all previous units that it outweighs the revenue from the additional unit. When MR < 0, TR is falling, and demand is inelastic (elasticity < 1). A rational profit-maximising monopolist would never produce in this range.
subject: economics

options.text: The firm is making a loss
Total Revenue is falling — selling more reduces total revenue
The firm should increase its price to zero

elimination_hints.hint: Negative MR doesn't necessarily mean the firm is making a loss. Loss depends on the relationship between TR and TC. Negative MR means TR is declining — the firm should reduce output, not that it is unprofitable overall.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing falling revenue (negative MR) with overall losses (TR < TC).

elimination_hints.hint: AR (price) is still positive even when MR is negative. AR can never be negative because price is always positive. AR = 0 would mean the product is free.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing negative MR with zero AR.

elimination_hints.hint: A price of zero means giving the product away for free. When MR is negative, the firm should REDUCE output (move back up the demand curve) to increase TR, not set price to zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding the corrective action when MR is negative.

question_text: The relationship between TR, MR, and price elasticity of demand (Ed) is:
explanation: When demand is elastic (Ed > 1): a price reduction increases TR because the percentage increase in quantity exceeds the percentage decrease in price. MR > 0. When demand is unit elastic (Ed = 1): TR is at its maximum. MR = 0. When demand is inelastic (Ed < 1): a price reduction decreases TR because the percentage increase in quantity is less than the percentage decrease in price. MR < 0. A monopolist always operates in the elastic portion (MR > 0, Ed > 1).
subject: economics

options.text: When Ed > 1, MR < 0 and TR falls
When Ed = 1, MR > 0 and TR rises
When Ed < 1, MR > 0 and TR rises
When Ed > 1, MR > 0; when Ed = 1, MR = 0 (TR max); when Ed < 1, MR < 0

elimination_hints.hint: This is reversed. When Ed > 1 (elastic), lowering price increases TR because quantity rises proportionally more. MR is POSITIVE. MR < 0 occurs when Ed < 1 (inelastic).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the elasticity-MR relationship.

elimination_hints.hint: When Ed = 1 (unit elastic), TR is at its MAXIMUM (not rising). MR = 0 at this point because TR is neither rising nor falling.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing TR at maximum (MR = 0) with TR still rising (MR > 0).

elimination_hints.hint: When Ed < 1 (inelastic), lowering price REDUCES TR because quantity doesn't rise enough to compensate. MR is NEGATIVE, not positive.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the inelastic demand result.
